title: SEGMENT 14 — Modern conditioning: antibodies replacing chemo

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — Modern conditioning: antibodies replacing chemo
I told you in Segment 7 that the dream of the field, for fifty years, has been to find a way to empty the marrow niches without the toxicity of chemotherapy and radiation.
We are, finally, beginning to approach that dream.
The technology is antibody-based conditioning, and it represents one of the most exciting frontiers in transplant medicine.
The concept is simple.

Instead of using systemic chemotherapy or radiation to kill bone marrow cells nonspecifically, you use a monoclonal antibody that binds specifically to a protein expressed on hematopoietic stem cells, and use that antibody to selectively eliminate the HSCs from their niches.

The antibody might be naked (relying on immune effector mechanisms to kill the targeted cells), or — more commonly in modern designs — it can be coupled to a cytotoxic payload, making it an antibody-drug conjugate or ADC.
The two main targets being pursued are CD117 and CD45.
CD117, also called c-Kit, is the receptor for stem cell factor and is highly expressed on hematopoietic stem cells.
An anti-CD117 antibody, alone or as an ADC, can selectively deplete HSCs from the marrow without the systemic toxicity of busulfan or melphalan.
CD45 is a pan-leukocyte marker, broader in its targeting, useful for combined depletion of HSCs and immune cells.
The leading company in this space has been Magenta Therapeutics, which developed anti-CD117 ADCs for transplant conditioning.

Although Magenta itself ran into financial difficulties, the concept has been picked up by multiple groups including Jasper Therapeutics, with anti-CD117 antibodies in clinical trials for conditioning ahead of HSCT — including for the gene therapy applications, where avoiding busulfan would be a major safety improvement, especially for pediatric sickle cell patients.
Why does this matter so much?
Because the conditioning toxicity is, in many cases, the biggest barrier to wider use of HSCT and HSCT-based gene therapies.
The kid with sickle cell who could be cured by Casgevy in principle faces a multi-month hospital stay, busulfan-induced infertility, and real treatment-related mortality risk.
Replace the busulfan with an outpatient infusion of an antibody-drug conjugate, and the procedure becomes dramatically safer.
This is the path to expanding gene therapy access from a few hundred patients per year to potentially tens of thousands.
Antibody-based conditioning is still mostly experimental in 2026, but it is the clearest near-term opportunity to remake the entire HSCT field.
Watch this space.
